metadata.title: Why a Probability Density Can Be Greater Than One
metadata.note: Clean build. Density carries inverse-length units; point probability is zero for a continuous variable; exact example P(|x|<=0.1 nm)=1-exp(-0.4)=0.3297.

source_of_truth: beat_sheet.json — edit the sheet, not this file

beats.label: This is Liam, in for Bear. A normalized wave function can have absolute psi squared equal to two at

beats.prompt: For a continuous position, absolute psi squared is a probability density, not a dimensionless probability at one exact point.
beats.label: For a continuous position, absolute psi squared is a probability density, not a dimensionless probab

beats.prompt: In one dimension its units are inverse length. A value of two per nanometer is not the number two as a probability.
beats.label: In one dimension its units are inverse length. A value of two per nanometer is not the number two as

beats.prompt: The probability for an interval is the integral of the density across that interval: an area, with the length units cancelling.
beats.label: The probability for an interval is the integral of the density across that interval: an area, with t

beats.prompt: Consider psi equals one over square root a times e to the minus absolute x over a. It is normalized on the whole line.
beats.label: Consider psi equals one over square root a times e to the minus absolute x over a. It is normalized

beats.prompt: Its density is one over a times e to the minus two absolute x over a. With a equal to 0.5 nanometers, the peak is two per nanometer.
beats.label: Its density is one over a times e to the minus two absolute x over a. With a equal to 0.5 nanometers

beats.prompt: Now ask for the particle within plus or minus 0.1 nanometers. The exact area is one minus e to the minus 0.4, about 0.330.
beats.label: Now ask for the particle within plus or minus 0.1 nanometers. The exact area is one minus e to the m

beats.prompt: The peak density exceeds one, yet the highlighted interval probability is safely below one. There is no contradiction.
beats.label: The peak density exceeds one, yet the highlighted interval probability is safely below one. There is

beats.prompt: For an ideal continuous variable, the probability of one exact point is zero. A finite detector always covers a nonzero interval or volume.
beats.label: For an ideal continuous variable, the probability of one exact point is zero. A finite detector alwa

beats.prompt: The density's numerical height also changes when you change units. Two per nanometer is two billion per meter, while every integrated probability stays unchange
beats.label: The density's numerical height also changes when you change units. Two per nanometer is two billion

beats.prompt: Population density works the same way. Ten thousand people per square kilometer is far above one; only density times area gives a count.
beats.label: Population density works the same way. Ten thousand people per square kilometer is far above one; on

beats.prompt: Never ask whether a continuous density is below one. Check its units, then integrate the region your measurement actually resolves.

Density may exceed one. Its integrated area may not.
